skill.name: find-skills
skill.description: Search multiple agent skill registries, compare popularity signals, check installed skills, and run heuristic safety scans on top candidates before recommending a skill.
skill.summary: Find relevant agent skills across skills.sh, Clawhub, and GitHub with popularity, install, and safety context.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, network, filesystem, secret, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found most severe secret and crypto alerts are scanner-rule strings or documentation examples, while the real behavior is a shell script that queries public registries, reads local installed-skill folder names, stores temporary results, and fetches candidate skill bodies for heuristic scanning. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, destructive file writes, or confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Public Registry Network Requests
medium_findings.description: The script sends the user query to public registry endpoints and fetches candidate skill bodies from public services. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it can disclose the search topic and depends on third-party responses.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are explicit curl requests to public registry and raw-content endpoints. The behavior is documented and purpose-aligned, but query disclosure and external content retrieval are real risks.

medium_findings.title: Local Installed Skill Directory Enumeration
medium_findings.description: The script reads folder names under local agent skill directories to mark already installed skills. It does not read arbitrary file contents, but it exposes local installed-skill names in the generated report.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly enumerates two hidden home-directory skill folders and stores the names. The scope is narrow and read-only, so the finding is moderate rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Downloaded Skill Body Heuristic Scanning
medium_findings.description: The script downloads candidate skill files or zip content and scans them with grep patterns. This supports the advertised safety check, but processing untrusted remote content can create reliability and review risks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner processes remote candidate content and archives, but only extracts SKILL.md and does not execute it. The risk is credible yet constrained by read-only handling.

low_findings.title: External Command Usage Appears Bounded
low_findings.description: The many external command findings are mostly normal shell tooling such as jq, curl, gh, grep, wc, sort, unzip, and printf. Arguments are generally quoted or derived from encoded query data, and no direct execution of user-provided shell text was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script is command-heavy, so the pattern is real, but semantic review did not find shell eval of user input. The remaining concern is operational complexity rather than confirmed injection.

low_findings.title: Secret Access Alerts Are Scanner Rule Text
low_findings.description: The reported secret and environment findings come from grep patterns that look for secret references inside downloaded candidate files. No evidence found that the skill reads SSH keys, AWS credentials, private keys, or .env file contents from the user machine.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious strings appear inside detection rules and documentation tables, not file-read operations. The only local home-directory access found is installed skill folder enumeration.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alerts Are Pattern Documentation
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and base64 findings reflect documented risky patterns searched by the scanner, not cryptographic operations performed by the skill. No evidence found of actual weak cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses grep to detect curl-pipe, eval-remote, and base64-pipe execution patterns in other skills. It does not decode or execute obfuscated payloads.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script with Network and Local Filesystem Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on a Bash script that combines public network calls, local directory enumeration, temporary files, and command-line tools. This combination deserves marketplace warning even though the observed intent is legitimate discovery and scanning.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The combination is confirmed in code and matches the static heuristic. Manual review lowers severity because the operations are purpose-aligned and no destructive or exfiltration behavior was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Heuristic Detection Rules Mention Sensitive Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains sensitive path strings in scanning rules and documentation so it can warn about risky third-party skills. These strings triggered static alerts but are not evidence that this skill reads those secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The context is a detector for risky candidate skills. I did not find a file-open, cat, upload, or network send operation for those sensitive paths.

content.value_statement: Finding the right agent skill is hard when each registry shows different results. This skill searches major sources together and adds popularity, install, and safety context.
actual_capabilities: Searches skills.sh, Clawhub, and GitHub skill topics from one command.
Ranks each source by its native popularity signals, such as installs or stars.
Checks local agent skill folders to flag skills already installed.
Fetches top candidate skill files and scans for risky installation or secret access patterns.
Produces formatted reports and optional machine-readable results for follow-up analysis.
Shows source health, latency, result counts, and candidate links.
limitations: Search quality depends on registry metadata and keyword matches.
The safety scan is heuristic and is not a full security audit.
GitHub results require the GitHub CLI and only cover topic-tagged repositories.
It does not install skills automatically or verify runtime behavior after installation.

use_cases.title: Pick a Task Skill
use_cases.description: Find a published skill for a task before asking the agent to build the workflow from scratch.

use_cases.title: Compare Marketplace Results
use_cases.description: Compare installs, stars, and registry coverage before recommending one skill to a team.

use_cases.title: Screen Candidate Skills
use_cases.description: Run a first-pass scan for risky patterns before manually reviewing a candidate skill.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Find a skill that helps with PDF form filling. Show the best option and one backup.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Adjacent Searches
prompt_templates.scenario: Broaden a search with related terms
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for skills related to frontend design, UI components, and design systems. Recommend the strongest match.

prompt_templates.title: Review Before Install
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate a candidate before adding it
prompt_templates.prompt: Find deployment skills for Vercel, scan the top candidates, and tell me which one is safest to install.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Shortlist
prompt_templates.prompt: Search all registries for Playwright testing skills, compare popularity and fit, and produce a ranked shortlist with risks.

output_examples.input: Find a skill for React performance work.
output_examples.output: A concise recommendation naming the best matching skill, its registry sources, popularity signals, safety badge, and one alternative.

output_examples.input: Search for video generation skills and scan the top five.
output_examples.output: A source-by-source report showing top results, registry health, risk badges, and links for manual review.

output_examples.input: Do I already have a useful documentation skill installed?
output_examples.output: A report that highlights matching installed skills and explains whether a new install is needed.

best_practices: Run two or three adjacent searches before deciding no good skill exists.
Read the candidate skill documentation before recommending or installing it.
Treat caution and risky scan flags as review prompts, not final proof.
anti_patterns: Do not recommend a skill from metadata alone without checking its documented scope.
Do not treat a clean heuristic scan as a complete security review.
Do not install a discovered community skill without explicit user approval.

faq.question: Does this skill install other skills automatically?
faq.answer: No. It finds candidates and shows install guidance, but installation remains a separate user-approved action.

faq.question: Which registries does it search?
faq.answer: It searches skills.sh, Clawhub, and GitHub repositories tagged with common agent skill topics.

faq.question: Does it need API keys?
faq.answer: No API keys are required. GitHub results can use the GitHub CLI if it is available.

faq.question: What does the safety scan check?
faq.answer: It checks candidate skill files for risky patterns such as remote shell installs, broad tool grants, and secret references.

faq.question: Can it miss useful skills?
faq.answer: Yes. Registries rely on names, summaries, and topics, so related search terms can reveal different results.

faq.question: Is it suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is documented for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
